question: Explain the purpose of a structure in C++ and describe the syntax used to declare one.
expectedPoints: A structure (struct) is a user-defined composite data type that groups together variables of different data types under a single name.
The declaration begins with the keyword `struct`, followed by the structure tag name, and then a block of member variable declarations inside curly braces `{}`.
The declaration is terminated with a semicolon after the closing brace.

question: Compare and contrast the declaration of a structure with the declaration of an array. Discuss two key differences in their purpose and usage.
expectedPoints: An array is a homogeneous collection, meaning all its elements must be of the same data type. A structure is a heterogeneous collection, allowing its members to be of different data types.
An array provides indexed access to its elements (e.g., `arr[0]`), while a structure provides named access to its members using the dot operator (e.g., `student.name`).
Arrays are typically used to represent lists of similar items, while structures are used to model a single entity with multiple attributes, like a student record with a name, ID, and GPA.

question: A program needs to manage an inventory of books. Each book has a title (string), author (string), ISBN (long), price (float), and a boolean indicating if it is in stock. Design the structure declaration for this scenario. Furthermore, explain the memory allocation process for an instance of this structure and how each member is accessed.
expectedPoints: The structure declaration: `struct Book { string title; string author; long ISBN; float price; bool inStock; };`

Memory allocation: When an instance of `Book` is created, a contiguous block of memory is allocated that is large enough to hold all its members (the size of two strings, a long, a float, and a bool, plus potential padding for alignment).
Member access: Each member variable is accessed using the dot operator (e.g., `book1.title`, `book1.ISBN`).
The strings (`title` and `author`) are objects themselves, so the structure contains the string objects, which in turn manage their own dynamic memory for the character data.
The explanation should demonstrate an understanding of the composite nature of the structure and the memory footprint of its constituent parts.
